column7: Description
column14: Report Objectives

column7: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 is an international standard for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an Information Security Management System (ISMS). It defines requirements for organizational context, leadership oversight, risk assessment and treatment processes, performance evaluation, and the implementation of security controls, including those listed in Annex A. Organizations select ISO 27001 when formalizing a structured security program, preparing for certification, or demonstrating systematic risk management to customers and regulators.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's Information Security Management System within the defined ISMS scope against ISO/IEC 27001:2022, including Clauses 4 through 10 and applicable Annex A controls, to assess conformity with mandatory requirements, effectiveness of risk assessment and risk treatment processes, adequacy of control selection and implementation, evidence of operational performance and monitoring, identification of nonconformities, and overall readiness for certification, surveillance, or continuous improvement.

column7: SOC 2 Type 1 is an AICPA attestation framework that evaluates whether a service organization�s controls are suitably designed to meet the Trust Services Criteria for Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, and Privacy as of a specific date. It focuses on control design rather than operating effectiveness over time. Organizations select SOC 2 Type 1 when preparing for customer assurance requirements or beginning a structured compliance program.
column14: This assessment evaluated the design of controls relevant to the applicable AICPA Trust Services Criteria for Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, and Privacy as of a specified point in time within the defined system boundary to determine whether controls were suitably designed to meet the stated control objectives and to identify design gaps or deficiencies requiring remediation prior to a formal SOC 2 Type 1 examination.

column7: SOC 2 Type 2 is an AICPA attestation framework that evaluates both the design and operating effectiveness of controls relevant to the Trust Services Criteria over a defined audit period. It assesses whether controls are consistently implemented and functioning as intended. Organizations select SOC 2 Type 2 to demonstrate sustained control performance and provide independent assurance to customers and stakeholders.
column14: This assessment evaluated both the design and operating effectiveness of controls relevant to the applicable AICPA Trust Services Criteria over the defined review period and system scope to determine whether controls were consistently implemented, evidenced, and functioning as intended, to identify deviations or control failures, and to assess overall readiness for a formal SOC 2 Type 2 examination.

column7: CIS Controls v8 Implementation Group 2 provides a prioritized set of cybersecurity safeguards designed for organizations facing moderate risk and complexity. It builds upon foundational safeguards and introduces additional controls focused on threat detection, vulnerability management, access control maturity, and operational security practices. Organizations select Implementation Group 2 to strengthen defensive capabilities beyond baseline security and align with widely recognized best practices.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's cybersecurity control environment against CIS Controls v8 Implementation Group 2 within the defined scope to assess coverage of prioritized safeguards for moderate-risk environments, maturity of control implementation, consistency of operational execution, alignment with threat-informed defense practices, and identification of control gaps requiring risk-based remediation.

column7: CIS Controls v8 Implementation Group 1 defines essential cybersecurity safeguards intended for organizations with limited resources or lower complexity environments. It emphasizes foundational protections such as asset inventory, secure configuration, access control, and basic vulnerability management. Organizations select Implementation Group 1 to establish a structured baseline of cyber hygiene aligned with common threat mitigation practices.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's foundational cybersecurity safeguards against CIS Controls v8 Implementation Group 1 within the defined scope to determine baseline control coverage, adequacy of essential technical and procedural safeguards, alignment with common threat mitigation practices, and identification of fundamental control deficiencies requiring remediation.

column7: NIST Cybersecurity Framework version 2.0 is a risk-based framework organized around six Core Functions: Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover. It provides outcome-focused guidance for managing cybersecurity risk and integrating governance, risk management, and operational security activities. Organizations select NIST CSF to assess overall cybersecurity maturity, align security strategy with enterprise risk management, and structure continuous improvement initiatives.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's cybersecurity program against the NIST Cybersecurity Framework v2.0 Core Functions of Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover within the defined scope to assess alignment with CSF outcomes and categories, evaluate governance integration and risk management maturity, identify systemic control gaps and risk concentrations, and provide a structured, risk-informed improvement roadmap.

column2: PCI DSS v4.0 - Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard
column7: PCI DSS v4.0 is a global security standard governing the protection of cardholder data within payment processing environments. It defines technical and procedural requirements related to network security, encryption, access control, vulnerability management, logging, and security monitoring. Organizations that store, process, or transmit payment card data select PCI DSS to assess compliance readiness and reduce risk within their cardholder data environment.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's cardholder data environment and related security controls against PCI DSS v4.0 requirements within the defined scope to assess implementation of technical and procedural safeguards, validation of control design and operational effectiveness, identification of requirement-level gaps, and readiness for formal assessment by a Qualified Security Assessor.

column7: CMMC Level 1 establishes foundational cybersecurity practices required for organizations handling Federal Contract Information. It aligns with basic safeguarding requirements derived from federal acquisition regulations and focuses on essential access control, identification, and system protection practices. Organizations select CMMC Level 1 to demonstrate compliance with minimum federal cybersecurity requirements and prepare for contractual assessments.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's implementation of CMMC Level 1 foundational practices within the defined scope to determine adherence to required security practices for protection of Federal Contract Information, assess control implementation consistency, identify unmet practice requirements, and evaluate readiness for third-party CMMC assessment.

column2: HIPAA Security Rule - Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act
column7: HIPAA establishes regulatory requirements for protecting the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of protected health information. It includes administrative, physical, and technical safeguard requirements under the Security Rule and privacy protections under the Privacy Rule. Healthcare providers, business associates, and related entities select HIPAA assessments to evaluate regulatory compliance, strengthen PHI protection controls, and reduce enforcement risk.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's administrative, physical, and technical safeguards for the protection of protected health information against applicable HIPAA Security Rule and related Privacy Rule requirements within the defined scope to assess regulatory alignment, effectiveness of risk analysis and risk management processes, identification of safeguard deficiencies, and exposure to compliance and enforcement risk.

column7: ISO/IEC 27001:2013 is the previous edition of the international standard for implementing and maintaining an Information Security Management System. It defines requirements for risk management, leadership oversight, performance evaluation, and control implementation based on Annex A safeguards. Organizations maintaining certification under the 2013 version or transitioning to the 2022 revision select this framework to assess conformity and support continued compliance.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's Information Security Management System within the defined ISMS scope against ISO/IEC 27001:2013, including Clauses 4 through 10 and applicable Annex A controls, to assess conformity with standard requirements, effectiveness of risk treatment activities, adequacy of control implementation and monitoring, identification of nonconformities, and readiness for certification or continued compliance.

column2: Financial Industry Regulatory Authority Rules and Standards
column7: FINRA rules and regulatory standards govern broker-dealers and securities firms operating in the United States. They establish requirements for supervisory controls, cybersecurity practices, recordkeeping, market conduct, and risk management oversight. Organizations subject to FINRA oversight select this framework to evaluate regulatory compliance readiness, strengthen supervisory systems, and prepare for examination or enforcement review.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's supervisory controls, compliance program, recordkeeping practices, cybersecurity safeguards, and operational governance against applicable FINRA rules and regulatory notices within the defined scope to assess adequacy of supervisory systems, effectiveness of control implementation, identification of rule-level gaps, and readiness for regulatory examination or enforcement scrutiny.

column7: The Bank Secrecy Act establishes anti-money laundering and financial crime prevention requirements for financial institutions. It mandates customer due diligence, transaction monitoring, suspicious activity reporting, and governance oversight controls. Organizations select this framework to assess AML program effectiveness, regulatory compliance posture, and exposure to financial crime enforcement risk.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's anti-money laundering program, customer due diligence controls, transaction monitoring mechanisms, suspicious activity reporting processes, and governance oversight against applicable Bank Secrecy Act requirements within the defined scope to determine regulatory alignment, effectiveness of detection and reporting controls, identification of compliance gaps, and exposure to financial crime and enforcement risk.

column7: Sarbanes-Oxley establishes requirements for internal controls over financial reporting, executive accountability, IT general controls, and audit oversight for publicly traded companies. It emphasizes documentation, segregation of duties, change management, and control testing. Organizations select SOX assessments to evaluate financial control design, identify material weaknesses, and prepare for external audit validation.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's internal controls over financial reporting, IT general controls, segregation of duties, change management, access governance, and executive certification processes against applicable Sarbanes-Oxley requirements within the defined scope to assess control design and operating effectiveness, identify material weaknesses or significant deficiencies, and support audit and regulatory readiness.

column7: 23 NYCRR 500 is a cybersecurity regulation issued by the New York Department of Financial Services that requires covered financial institutions to implement a risk-based cybersecurity program, governance oversight, incident reporting procedures, and third-party risk management controls. Organizations select this framework to assess regulatory compliance readiness and strengthen cybersecurity governance in alignment with DFS requirements.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's cybersecurity program, governance structure, risk assessment methodology, incident response capability, third-party risk management practices, and technical safeguards against 23 NYCRR 500 requirements within the defined scope to assess regulatory alignment, identify control and documentation gaps, and determine readiness for Department of Financial Services examination and certification obligations.

column2: NIST SP 800-171 - Protecting CUI in Nonfederal Systems
column7: NIST SP 800-171 defines security requirements for protecting Controlled Unclassified Information in nonfederal systems and organizations. It outlines 110 security requirements across fourteen control families covering access control, configuration management, incident response, and system integrity. Organizations handling federal data select this framework to evaluate compliance readiness and safeguard CUI under contractual obligations.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's implementation of the 110 security requirements across the fourteen control families of NIST SP 800-171 within the defined scope to assess protection of Controlled Unclassified Information, validate control implementation and documentation, identify unmet requirements, and determine readiness for contractual attestation or assessment.

column2: FFIEC - Federal Financial Institutions Examination Council Guidelines
column7: FFIEC guidance provides supervisory expectations for cybersecurity, IT governance, risk management, business continuity, and third-party oversight within financial institutions. It is used by federal banking regulators during examinations. Organizations select this framework to evaluate alignment with supervisory expectations and strengthen examination readiness.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's governance, risk management, cybersecurity controls, and operational resilience practices against applicable FFIEC handbooks and guidance within the defined scope to assess alignment with supervisory expectations, identify examination exposure areas, and evaluate overall readiness for regulatory review.

column2: SEC - U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission Cyber Rules
column7: SEC cybersecurity rules establish requirements for risk management, incident disclosure, governance oversight, and board accountability for public companies and certain regulated entities. Organizations select this framework to assess disclosure readiness, governance maturity, and compliance with federal securities cybersecurity reporting obligations.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's cybersecurity risk management program, incident response governance, disclosure controls, board oversight processes, and material incident reporting mechanisms against applicable SEC cybersecurity rules within the defined scope to assess regulatory alignment, identify disclosure or governance gaps, and determine readiness for enforcement and reporting obligations.

column7: FedRAMP Low establishes a standardized security baseline for cloud systems processing low-impact federal information. Derived from NIST SP 800-53 controls, it defines minimum security requirements for authorization to operate. Organizations select this baseline to assess readiness for federal cloud authorization and align security controls with government expectations.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's cloud system controls against the FedRAMP Low Impact Baseline derived from NIST SP 800-53 within the defined authorization boundary to assess implementation of required security controls, adequacy of documentation artifacts, identification of control gaps, and readiness for authorization to operate.

column7: FedRAMP Moderate establishes a more rigorous security baseline for cloud systems processing moderate-impact federal information. It includes expanded control requirements, continuous monitoring expectations, and formal authorization processes. Organizations select this baseline to evaluate cloud security maturity and prepare for authorization within federal environments.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's cloud system controls against the FedRAMP Moderate Impact Baseline derived from NIST SP 800-53 within the defined authorization boundary to assess implementation and operational effectiveness of required controls, sufficiency of continuous monitoring processes, identification of control deficiencies, and readiness for authorization to operate.

column7: FedRAMP High defines the most stringent security baseline for cloud systems supporting high-impact federal information. It requires extensive control implementation, documentation, and continuous monitoring practices. Organizations select this baseline to assess advanced cloud security capabilities and prepare for authorization in high-risk federal environments.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's cloud system controls against the FedRAMP High Impact Baseline derived from NIST SP 800-53 within the defined authorization boundary to assess implementation rigor, documentation completeness, continuous monitoring maturity, identification of high-impact control gaps, and readiness for authorization to operate in high-risk environments.

column2: NFA - National Futures Association Compliance Rules
column7: NFA rules govern futures commission merchants, commodity trading advisors, and related entities. They establish supervisory, cybersecurity, and compliance oversight requirements. Organizations select this framework to assess regulatory alignment, strengthen supervisory controls, and prepare for examination or enforcement review.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's supervisory procedures, cybersecurity controls, compliance governance, and recordkeeping practices against applicable NFA compliance rules within the defined scope to assess regulatory alignment, identify supervisory or control deficiencies, and determine readiness for examination and enforcement review.

column2: FTC Safeguards Rule - Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act Compliance
column7: The FTC Safeguards Rule implements the information security provisions of GLBA and requires covered financial institutions to maintain a written information security program, conduct risk assessments, implement safeguards, and oversee service providers. Organizations select this framework to evaluate data protection controls and regulatory compliance readiness.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's written information security program, risk assessment processes, administrative and technical safeguards, service provider oversight, and board-level reporting mechanisms against the FTC Safeguards Rule requirements under GLBA within the defined scope to assess compliance alignment, identify control gaps, and determine readiness for regulatory enforcement review.

column7: GLBA establishes privacy and safeguard requirements for financial institutions handling consumer financial information. It governs privacy notices, data sharing limitations, and protection of nonpublic personal information. Organizations select this framework to assess privacy compliance posture and strengthen customer data protection controls.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's privacy notices, data sharing practices, information security safeguards, and oversight controls against applicable Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act requirements within the defined scope to assess regulatory alignment, identify privacy and safeguard deficiencies, and evaluate exposure to enforcement and reputational risk.

column7: GDPR is the European Union regulation governing personal data protection, data subject rights, cross-border transfers, breach notification, and accountability obligations. It applies to organizations processing EU personal data regardless of location. Organizations select GDPR assessments to evaluate compliance readiness, strengthen privacy governance, and reduce regulatory fine exposure.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's data processing activities, lawful basis determinations, data subject rights management processes, security safeguards, cross-border transfer mechanisms, and governance structures against GDPR requirements within the defined scope to assess compliance alignment, identify accountability and documentation gaps, and determine exposure to regulatory fines and enforcement risk.

column2: CMMC Level 2 - Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification
column7: CMMC Level 2 establishes advanced cybersecurity requirements for organizations handling Controlled Unclassified Information within the U.S. defense industrial base. It aligns with NIST SP 800-171 and requires formal third-party assessment. Organizations select CMMC Level 2 to evaluate implementation maturity and prepare for certification under Department of Defense contracts.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's implementation and institutionalization of CMMC Level 2 practices mapped to NIST SP 800-171 requirements within the defined scope to assess protection of Controlled Unclassified Information, validate maturity of process implementation, identify unmet practices, and determine readiness for certified third-party assessment.

column7: The NIST SP 800-53 High Impact Baseline defines a comprehensive set of security and privacy controls for systems where loss of confidentiality, integrity, or availability would have severe impact. Organizations select this baseline to evaluate high-risk system protections and align with federal security expectations.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's security and privacy controls against the NIST SP 800-53 High Impact Baseline within the defined system boundary to assess implementation rigor, control effectiveness, documentation completeness, identification of high-impact control gaps, and overall risk posture in environments requiring maximum protection.

column7: NIST SP 800-53 provides a comprehensive catalog of security and privacy controls used across federal and enterprise environments. Organizations select the full catalog to tailor control sets based on system impact, regulatory requirements, or internal risk models.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's selected security and privacy controls against the full NIST SP 800-53 control catalog within the defined system scope to assess control selection rationale, implementation effectiveness, documentation alignment, identification of control deficiencies, and overall risk management maturity.

column7: CCPA establishes consumer data privacy rights for California residents, including access, deletion, opt-out, and transparency requirements. It governs data collection, disclosure, and service provider obligations. Organizations select CCPA assessments to evaluate consumer privacy compliance readiness and reduce regulatory and civil liability risk.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's consumer data collection practices, notice disclosures, data subject request handling processes, information security safeguards, and service provider oversight mechanisms against CCPA requirements within the defined scope to assess compliance alignment, identify privacy control gaps, and determine exposure to regulatory enforcement and civil liability.

column7: ISO 9001 is an international standard for establishing and maintaining a Quality Management System focused on consistent product and service delivery. It defines requirements for leadership accountability, process control, risk-based thinking, performance measurement, corrective action, and continual improvement. Organizations select ISO 9001 to formalize operational quality practices, improve customer satisfaction, and prepare for certification.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's Quality Management System within the defined scope against ISO 9001 requirements, including leadership commitment, process design and control, risk-based thinking, performance evaluation, corrective action, and continual improvement mechanisms, to assess conformity with Clauses 4 through 10, identify nonconformities and process control gaps, and determine readiness for certification or surveillance audit.

column7: ISO 22301:2019 establishes requirements for a Business Continuity Management System designed to ensure organizational resilience during disruptive events. It covers business impact analysis, risk assessment, continuity strategy development, incident response integration, testing and exercising, and continual improvement. Organizations select ISO 22301 to strengthen operational resilience and prepare for certification or regulatory scrutiny.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's Business Continuity Management System within the defined scope against ISO 22301:2019 requirements, including business impact analysis, risk assessment, continuity strategy development, incident response integration, exercising and testing, and continual improvement processes, to assess conformity with standard requirements, identify capability and documentation gaps, and determine operational resilience and certification readiness.

column7: ISO/IEC 27035 provides guidance for establishing and improving structured information security incident management processes. It outlines incident detection, reporting, assessment, response coordination, communication, and post-incident learning practices. Organizations select ISO 27035 to mature their incident response capability and align with internationally recognized lifecycle management standards.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's incident management framework against ISO/IEC 27035 guidance within the defined scope to assess preparedness across detection, reporting, assessment, response, communication, and post-incident learning activities, determine alignment with structured incident lifecycle management practices, identify procedural and governance gaps, and evaluate overall incident response maturity.

column7: ITIL is a globally recognized framework for managing IT services across their lifecycle, including service strategy, design, transition, operation, and continual improvement. It emphasizes structured processes such as change enablement, incident and problem management, service level management, and performance optimization. Organizations select ITIL to improve service reliability, operational efficiency, and governance alignment.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's IT service management practices against ITIL guidance within the defined scope, including service strategy, service design, change enablement, incident and problem management, service level management, and continual improvement processes, to assess operational maturity, governance alignment, process effectiveness, and opportunities to improve service performance and risk control.

column7: The COSO Internal Control Integrated Framework provides principles for designing and evaluating enterprise internal controls. It defines five components: control environment, risk assessment, control activities, information and communication, and monitoring. Organizations select COSO to assess governance effectiveness, strengthen internal controls over operations and reporting, and support regulatory or audit requirements.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's internal control environment against the COSO Internal Control Integrated Framework within the defined scope, including control environment, risk assessment, control activities, information and communication, and monitoring components, to assess design and operating effectiveness of controls, identify material weaknesses or significant deficiencies, and evaluate overall governance and risk management maturity.

column7: COBIT 2019 is a governance and management framework for enterprise information and technology. It aligns IT objectives with business goals, defines governance and management objectives, and supports performance measurement and capability assessment. Organizations select COBIT to evaluate IT governance maturity, strengthen oversight, and optimize value delivery and risk management.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's IT governance and management system against COBIT 2019 within the defined scope, including governance objectives, management objectives, performance management practices, and alignment with enterprise goals, to assess capability levels, identify governance and control gaps, and determine effectiveness of IT value delivery and risk optimization.

column7: ISO/IEC 42001 establishes requirements for an Artificial Intelligence Management System focused on responsible development, deployment, and oversight of AI technologies. It addresses governance structure, risk assessment, lifecycle controls, transparency, monitoring, and continual improvement. Organizations select ISO 42001 to formalize AI governance practices and prepare for emerging regulatory and ethical expectations.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's Artificial Intelligence Management System within the defined scope against ISO/IEC 42001 requirements, including AI governance structure, risk assessment methodology, lifecycle controls, transparency and accountability mechanisms, monitoring processes, and continual improvement practices, to assess conformity with management system requirements, identify control and oversight gaps, and determine readiness for certification and responsible AI deployment.

column2: ISO/IEC 27003 - Information Security Management System Guidance
column7: ISO/IEC 27003 provides implementation guidance for establishing and maintaining an Information Security Management System aligned with ISO 27001 requirements. It offers structured recommendations for risk assessment, control selection, governance integration, and performance evaluation. Organizations select ISO 27003 to support structured ISMS development and improve readiness for ISO 27001 certification.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's implementation and operationalization of its Information Security Management System against ISO/IEC 27003 guidance within the defined scope to assess adequacy of ISMS design, alignment with ISO 27001 structural requirements, effectiveness of risk assessment and control selection processes, and identification of structural or governance deficiencies impacting ISMS maturity.

column2: NIST AI RMF - Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework
column7: The NIST Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework provides guidance for managing risks associated with AI systems through four core functions: Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage. It emphasizes transparency, accountability, bias mitigation, security, and lifecycle oversight. Organizations select NIST AI RMF to assess AI-related risks, strengthen governance controls, and align with evolving regulatory and industry expectations.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's AI governance and risk management practices against the NIST AI Risk Management Framework within the defined scope, including Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage functions, to assess identification and mitigation of AI-related risks, transparency and accountability mechanisms, lifecycle risk management integration, and overall maturity of responsible AI oversight practices.

column2: U.S. Food and Drug Administration Regulations
column7: U.S. Food and Drug Administration regulations govern quality systems, product safety, data integrity, validation controls, and regulatory documentation for organizations operating in regulated life sciences and medical industries. They include current Good Manufacturing Practice requirements and inspection oversight expectations. Organizations select this framework to assess regulatory compliance readiness and strengthen quality and documentation controls prior to FDA inspection.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's quality systems, validation controls, data integrity practices, change management processes, and regulatory documentation against applicable U.S. Food and Drug Administration regulations within the defined scope to assess compliance with current Good Manufacturing Practices and related requirements, identify procedural and control deficiencies, and determine readiness for regulatory inspection and enforcement scrutiny.

column7: Federal Communications Commission rules establish regulatory requirements for telecommunications providers and related entities, including operational compliance, reporting obligations, cybersecurity safeguards, and consumer protection standards. Organizations select this framework to evaluate regulatory alignment, strengthen compliance monitoring mechanisms, and prepare for supervisory review or enforcement exposure.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's operational, technical, cybersecurity, and reporting controls against applicable Federal Communications Commission rules within the defined scope to assess regulatory alignment, adequacy of compliance monitoring mechanisms, identification of rule-level gaps, and readiness for supervisory review or enforcement action.

column7: The CAMELS rating system is used by U.S. banking regulators to assess financial institutions across Capital adequacy, Asset quality, Management effectiveness, Earnings performance, Liquidity management, and Sensitivity to market risk. It provides a supervisory evaluation model rather than a prescriptive control framework. Institutions select CAMELS-based assessments to evaluate examination readiness, governance maturity, and overall institutional resilience.
column14: This assessment evaluated governance practices, capital adequacy controls, asset quality oversight, management effectiveness, earnings controls, liquidity management, and sensitivity to market risk within the defined scope to assess alignment with CAMELS supervisory rating criteria, identify areas that may adversely impact examination outcomes, and evaluate overall institutional resilience and supervisory readiness.

column2: UNECE R155 & R156 - Vehicle Cybersecurity & Software Updates
column7: UNECE R155 and R156 establish regulatory requirements for automotive cybersecurity management systems and secure software update processes. They require lifecycle risk management, vulnerability monitoring, incident response capabilities, and controlled update mechanisms for connected vehicles. Automotive manufacturers and suppliers select this framework to assess regulatory compliance readiness and support type approval within applicable jurisdictions.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's vehicle cybersecurity management system and software update management system against UNECE R155 and R156 requirements within the defined scope to assess governance of automotive cyber risks, lifecycle security controls, vulnerability monitoring, secure software update mechanisms, and readiness for type approval and regulatory compliance.

column2: Cyber Essentials Plus - UK Government Cybersecurity Certification
column7: Cyber Essentials Plus is a UK government-backed cybersecurity certification that validates implementation and operational effectiveness of baseline technical controls, including secure configuration, access control, malware protection, and patch management. It requires independent technical verification. Organizations select Cyber Essentials Plus to demonstrate externally validated cyber hygiene and meet UK public sector or supply chain requirements.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's implementation and operational effectiveness of required technical controls under the UK Cyber Essentials Plus scheme within the defined scope to assess conformance with baseline security requirements, validate configuration and control effectiveness through testing, identify technical deficiencies, and determine readiness for certification assessment.

column2: Cyber Essentials - UK Government Cybersecurity Baseline
column7: Cyber Essentials defines a foundational set of technical cybersecurity controls intended to protect against common internet-based threats. It focuses on essential safeguards such as firewalls, secure configuration, user access control, malware protection, and patch management. Organizations select Cyber Essentials to establish baseline security maturity and prepare for certification.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's implementation of required baseline technical safeguards under the UK Cyber Essentials framework within the defined scope to assess compliance with core control requirements, identify foundational security gaps, and determine readiness for certification.

column2: CJIS - Criminal Justice Information Services Security Policy
column7: The FBI CJIS Security Policy defines administrative, technical, and physical safeguards required to protect Criminal Justice Information. It includes access control, encryption, logging, personnel screening, incident response, and audit requirements for agencies and service providers handling law enforcement data. Organizations select CJIS assessments to evaluate compliance readiness and strengthen protection of sensitive justice-related information.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's administrative, technical, and physical safeguards protecting Criminal Justice Information against the FBI CJIS Security Policy within the defined scope to assess compliance with access control, encryption, logging, personnel screening, and incident response requirements, identify control deficiencies, and determine readiness for CJIS audit or agency review.

column2: NIS2 - EU Network and Information Security Directive
column7: NIS2 establishes cybersecurity and resilience obligations for essential and important entities operating within the European Union. It requires risk management controls, supply chain oversight, incident reporting, executive accountability, and supervisory cooperation. Organizations select NIS2 assessments to evaluate regulatory readiness and strengthen cybersecurity governance in line with EU expectations.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's cybersecurity governance, risk management practices, incident reporting processes, supply chain security controls, and executive accountability mechanisms against NIS2 requirements within the defined scope to assess regulatory alignment, identify systemic control gaps, and determine readiness for supervisory authority oversight and enforcement.

column7: DORA establishes EU regulatory requirements for digital operational resilience within the financial sector. It defines obligations related to ICT risk management, incident reporting, resilience testing, third-party oversight, and governance accountability. Financial entities select DORA assessments to evaluate compliance readiness and enhance operational resilience under EU supervisory frameworks.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's ICT risk management framework, incident reporting mechanisms, digital operational resilience testing, third-party risk oversight, and governance structures against DORA requirements within the defined scope to assess compliance alignment, identify operational resilience deficiencies, and determine readiness for regulatory supervision and enforcement.

column2: HIPAA Privacy Rule - Health Information Privacy Requirements
column7: The HIPAA Privacy Rule establishes standards governing the use, disclosure, and protection of protected health information. It defines requirements for data subject rights, minimum necessary use, notice of privacy practices, administrative safeguards, and workforce oversight. Covered entities and business associates select this framework to assess compliance readiness and strengthen patient data privacy governance.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's policies, procedures, notice of privacy practices, data use and disclosure controls, minimum necessary standards, data subject rights handling processes, and workforce training mechanisms against HIPAA Privacy Rule requirements within the defined scope to assess regulatory alignment, identify privacy governance gaps, and determine exposure to enforcement and civil liability risk.

column7: The ISACA Quantum Computing Preparedness Framework provides structured guidance for assessing organizational exposure to emerging quantum computing risks, particularly those affecting cryptographic systems and long-term data protection. It emphasizes cryptographic inventory, algorithm dependency analysis, governance oversight, and transition planning toward post-quantum security. Organizations select this framework to evaluate future-state cryptographic resilience and develop a proactive quantum risk strategy.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's cryptographic governance structure, algorithm inventory, dependency mapping, key management practices, and transition planning capabilities against the ISACA Quantum Computing Preparedness Framework within the defined scope to assess exposure to quantum-era cryptographic risk, readiness for post-quantum migration, and maturity of long-term cryptographic resilience strategy.

column2: Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act
column7: FERPA is a U.S. federal law governing the privacy and protection of student education records. It establishes requirements for consent management, access controls, disclosure limitations, and record handling practices for educational institutions and service providers. Organizations select FERPA assessments to evaluate compliance readiness and strengthen governance over student data protection.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's policies, consent management processes, disclosure controls, access safeguards, and record retention practices against applicable FERPA requirements within the defined scope to assess compliance with statutory privacy obligations, identify administrative and technical control gaps, and evaluate exposure to regulatory and reputational risk.

column7: The Investment Advisers Act of 1940 establishes regulatory requirements for investment advisory firms, including fiduciary obligations, supervisory procedures, recordkeeping, and compliance oversight. It also intersects with cybersecurity and operational risk management expectations. Organizations select this framework to assess regulatory compliance posture and strengthen supervisory and governance controls.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's fiduciary oversight mechanisms, supervisory procedures, compliance monitoring controls, cybersecurity safeguards, and recordkeeping practices against applicable provisions of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 within the defined scope to assess regulatory alignment, control effectiveness, and exposure to examination or enforcement action.

column2: ISO/IEC 45001 - Occupational Health and Safety Management
column7: ISO 45001 is an international standard for implementing an Occupational Health and Safety Management System. It defines requirements for hazard identification, risk assessment, leadership commitment, operational controls, incident investigation, and continual improvement. Organizations select ISO 45001 to formalize workplace safety governance and prepare for certification.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's occupational health and safety management system within the defined scope against ISO 45001 requirements, including hazard identification, risk assessment processes, leadership commitment, operational controls, incident investigation, and continual improvement mechanisms, to assess conformity with management system requirements, identify nonconformities, and determine readiness for certification or surveillance audit.

column7: ISO 14001 establishes requirements for an Environmental Management System focused on identifying environmental aspects and impacts, managing compliance obligations, implementing operational controls, monitoring performance, and driving continual improvement. Organizations select ISO 14001 to strengthen environmental governance and prepare for certification or regulatory review.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's environmental management system within the defined scope against ISO 14001 requirements, including environmental aspects and impacts identification, compliance obligations management, operational controls, monitoring and measurement activities, and continual improvement processes, to assess conformity with management system requirements and readiness for certification.

column7: Environmental, Social, and Governance compliance frameworks focus on structured oversight of sustainability practices, ethical governance, risk transparency, and stakeholder reporting obligations. They address environmental impact management, social responsibility controls, and board-level governance mechanisms. Organizations select ESG compliance assessments to evaluate reporting readiness, strengthen governance maturity, and align with investor and regulatory expectations.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's ESG governance framework, sustainability reporting controls, environmental and social risk management processes, and board-level oversight mechanisms within the defined scope to assess alignment with applicable regulatory, stakeholder, and disclosure requirements, identify reporting and governance gaps, and evaluate overall ESG program maturity.

column7: Environment, Health, and Safety compliance frameworks address statutory obligations related to workplace safety, environmental protection, operational risk controls, and incident reporting. They emphasize regulatory alignment, documentation management, and continuous monitoring of safety performance. Organizations select EHS compliance assessments to evaluate regulatory readiness and reduce operational and legal risk exposure.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's environment, health, and safety compliance controls, operational safeguards, regulatory reporting mechanisms, and incident management processes within the defined scope to assess adherence to applicable statutory requirements, identify procedural and control deficiencies, and evaluate overall compliance and risk management effectiveness.

column7: Electronic Data Collection compliance frameworks govern the secure and lawful collection, storage, processing, and transmission of electronically captured data. They emphasize consent management, data integrity, access controls, retention policies, and regulatory documentation practices. Organizations select EDC assessments to evaluate compliance posture and strengthen governance over digitally collected information.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's electronic data collection processes, consent mechanisms, data integrity safeguards, storage and transmission controls, and regulatory documentation practices within the defined scope to assess compliance with applicable data collection regulations, identify security and privacy control gaps, and evaluate exposure to regulatory and operational risk.

column7: Zero Trust is a cybersecurity architecture model based on the principle of continuous verification and least-privilege access rather than implicit trust within network boundaries. It emphasizes identity-centric access controls, micro-segmentation, device validation, continuous monitoring, and policy enforcement across users and systems. Organizations select Zero Trust assessments to evaluate architectural maturity and modernize security posture against evolving threat models.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's cybersecurity architecture against Zero Trust principles within the defined scope, including identity-centric access controls, continuous authentication and authorization, network segmentation, device posture validation, data protection mechanisms, and monitoring capabilities, to assess architectural maturity, alignment with least-privilege and verification-based access models, and identification of structural security gaps.

column7: NIST SP 800-53 includes a comprehensive catalog of security and privacy controls used to protect information systems across federal and enterprise environments. The privacy controls address data minimization, consent, accountability, transparency, and governance oversight. Organizations select this framework to evaluate structured privacy control implementation and align with federal or enterprise risk management standards.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's implementation of privacy controls derived from NIST SP 800-53 within the defined system boundary to assess compliance with privacy engineering principles, adequacy of data minimization and consent mechanisms, effectiveness of governance and oversight processes, and identification of privacy-related control deficiencies.

column2: PCI DSS - Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard
column7: PCI DSS establishes global security requirements for protecting cardholder data within payment processing environments. It defines technical and procedural safeguards related to network segmentation, encryption, access control, monitoring, vulnerability management, and security governance. Organizations that store, process, or transmit payment card data select PCI DSS to evaluate compliance readiness and reduce payment-related data risk.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's cardholder data environment and supporting security controls against PCI DSS requirements within the defined scope to assess network segmentation, access control mechanisms, encryption safeguards, logging and monitoring capabilities, vulnerability management processes, and overall readiness for Qualified Security Assessor validation.

column7: The NIST Cybersecurity Framework provides a risk-based structure for managing cybersecurity programs through five Core Functions: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover. It emphasizes integration of security into enterprise risk management and supports maturity assessment and continuous improvement. Organizations select NIST CSF to evaluate overall cybersecurity posture and structure risk management initiatives.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's cybersecurity program against the NIST Cybersecurity Framework Core Functions of Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover within the defined scope to assess alignment with framework outcomes, maturity of risk management integration, identification of systemic control gaps, and development of a risk-informed improvement roadmap.

column7: CIS Controls v8 Implementation Group 3 provides advanced safeguards designed for organizations with complex environments or elevated threat exposure. It builds upon foundational controls and introduces enhanced capabilities in detection, response, and defensive depth. Organizations select Implementation Group 3 to assess higher-order control maturity and strengthen security posture in high-risk or regulated environments.
column14: This assessment evaluated the organization's cybersecurity control environment against CIS Controls v8 Implementation Group 3 within the defined scope to assess advanced safeguard implementation, integration of defensive controls across complex environments, operational consistency, threat-informed coverage, and identification of higher-order control gaps requiring strategic remediation.
